meta.title: E82 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 1 Series E82 engine — N47, N52, N54, N55. Real UK costs, reliability data and common failures. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series E82 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E82 engine typically costs £1,800–£6,500 supply only, depending on engine — the N47 diesel sits at £3,200–£5,500, while the N54 twin-turbo runs £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series E82 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 (125i/128i) — BMW's last naturally aspirated straight-six in the 1 Series. It delivers linear, smooth power without the turbocharging complexity of the N54/N55 or the timing-chain risk of the N47 diesel. VANOS and Valvetronic are the only real wear items.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series E82?

acceptedAnswer.text: N47 diesel timing chain stretch (the defining E82 diesel risk), N54 HPFP and injector failure on the 135i, N52 VANOS solenoid and cooling system wear on the 125i, and — unrelated to the engine — rear subframe mount cracking on high-mileage cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series E82 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N52 petrol six is the most reliable option — simple, linear, no turbo complexity. The N47 diesel carries significant timing-chain risk — check history before purchase. The N54 is brilliant but demanding — budget for HPFP and injector maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series E82 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre-2010 N54 135i without documented HPFP history is the one we consistently advise against. A pre-2013 N47 diesel without timing-chain history is a close second. The 123d twin-turbo diesel shares the N47 chain risk, so apply the same caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E82 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N47), long cranking or limp mode (N54 HPFP), rough idle or engine warning lights (N52 VANOS), or blue smoke and rough idle (any high-mileage engine) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series E82 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £10,000 E82, a £4,000 N47 recon is 40% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon brushes our 70% BMW threshold — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 1 Series E82 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 petrol six can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The N47 diesel's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The N54's lifespan is heavily dependent on fuel system maintenance — with proper care, it can also reach high mileages.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E82, E81, E87 and E88?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the first-generation 1 Series platform. The E82 is the 2-door coupé (2007–2013, this page). The E81 is the 3-door hatchback, the E87 is the 5-door hatchback, and the E88 is the 2-door convertible. Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 1 Series E82 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on N47/N52/N54/N55 units. Tell us your variant (118i, 120i, 125i, 128i, 135i, 118d, 120d, 123d or 1M) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 1 Series E82 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E82 coupé — from the N47 twin-turbo diesel to the N54 and N55 straight-sixes that powered the 135i and 1M. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the high-pressure fuel pump risk that still haunts early turbocharged cars. Backed by 240+ real UK E82 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series E82 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E82 coupé — from the efficient four-cylinder diesels to the iconic naturally aspirated and turbocharged straight-sixes.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N47D20C: 95% confidence, inherited directly from the Model Page's BMW-VERIFIED figures. N52, N54, N55: Expert-knowledge tier, inherited from the Model Page's pattern-data-tier ratings. N46, N43, N47D20D: Provisional-newly-assigned tier — no dedicated Engine Page exists for these codes yet; ratings derived from cross-referencing specialist and owner sources.

overview.intro: The E82 is the coupé body of BMW's first-generation 1 Series, launched in 2007 as a two-door, four-seat sports coupé. It shared its platform with the E81/E87 hatchbacks and E88 convertible, but introduced a longer wheelbase and a lower, wider stance. The engine range spanned from efficient four-cylinder petrol and diesel units to the range-topping N54 twin-turbo straight-six in the 135i and the limited-production 1 Series M Coupé. A 2010 Life Cycle Impulse (LCI) brought revised styling and the replacement of the N54 with the single-turbo N55 in the 135i.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E8x (shared with E81/E87/E88) • Predecessor: None (1 Series coupé was a new body style) • Successor: BMW 2 Series F22 (2014–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: E81 3-door hatchback, E87 5-door hatchback, E88 convertible
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E82 generated 240 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the N47 diesel and N54/N55 petrol units as these cars age into enthusiast ownership and maintenance cycles.

items.quote: The 123d is the pick of the E82 diesel range — 204 bhp and 400 Nm of torque from a twin-turbo four-cylinder that delivers genuine performance without the six-cylinder's fuel consumption. It's the diesel that feels like a petrol, and it's the one enthusiasts choose.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want diesel economy with near-petrol performance.

items.quote: The N52 is the last of BMW's naturally aspirated straight-sixes in the 1 Series — 215–230 bhp, linear power delivery, and none of the turbocharging complexity that defines the N54 and N55. VANOS and Valvetronic are the only real wear items.
items.whoItsFor: Enthusiasts who want classic BMW six-cylinder character without turbo anxiety.

items.quote: The N54 was BMW's first mass-production turbocharged direct-injection petrol engine. High-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure leads to long cranks and limp mode; injector and wastegate issues often follow. It's a brilliant engine when it works, but it demands deep pockets.
items.whoItsFor: 135i buyers who know the risks and have a maintenance budget.

items.quote: The N54's high-pressure fuel pump is its Achilles heel. Replacement costs run £800–£1,500 at a specialist, and if injectors fail alongside it, the bill climbs quickly. A full engine replacement on an N54 135i can exceed £6,500.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want 300+ bhp and are prepared for the maintenance.

items.quote: The 120d delivers 177 bhp, 350 Nm of torque, and genuine 50+ mpg economy — the best all-rounder in the E82 range. The N47's timing-chain risk is well-documented, but a car with chain history is the smartest buy in the range.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want performance, economy and value in one package.

items.quote: The 125i is often overshadowed by the 135i's power and the diesels' economy, but it's the sweet spot — 215 bhp, rear-wheel drive, and BMW's last naturally aspirated straight-six. It's the E82 that drives like a classic BMW should.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want the purest E82 driving experience.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice — simple four-cylinder, cheap to run
Best Petrol — reliable, linear, no turbo worries
Most Expensive — fast, tunable, but costly to maintain
Solid Choice — improved over N54, still has risks
Risk vs Reward — budget for chain history or the job itself
Best Diesel — twin-turbo performance, chain risk applies

economicsRule.title: The E82 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £5,000–£12,000 E82 sits at 27–110% of the car's value. On a sub-£6,000 E82, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £10,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 32–55% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E82 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 125i (N52) — simple naturally aspirated six, cheap to maintain, no turbo risks.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E82 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 135i (N54) — HPFP, injectors and turbos add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E82 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 120d (N47) with documented chain history — strong performance, great economy, most common variant.

problems.title: N47 Diesel Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The N47's rear-mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time. Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. The 123d's twin-turbo variant shares the same chain design, so the risk applies equally.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E82 worth under £6,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your E82 120d →

problems.title: N54 HPFP & Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The N54's high-pressure fuel pump suffers from internal wear leading to fuel pressure loss, hard starting, and eventual engine damage if ignored. Injector issues frequently follow HPFP failure, and wastegate rattle on the turbos is a separate but common complaint.
problems.recommendation: If your 135i has no documented HPFP history, treat it as overdue preventative maintenance — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows a lean-running failure.

cta.label: Compare N54 engine prices for your E82 135i →

problems.title: N52 VANOS Solenoid & Cooling System Wear
problems.rootCause: The N52's VANOS solenoids can become clogged with debris, causing rough idle, reduced power, and engine warning lights. The cooling system — water pump, thermostat and expansion tank — is also a known weak point as the plastic components age.
problems.recommendation: Have the VANOS solenoids cleaned or replaced at the first sign of rough running — a £300–£600 repair is cheap insurance. Replace the water pump and thermostat proactively at 80,000 miles to avoid overheating.

cta.label: Compare N52 engine prices for your E82 125i →

problems.title: E82 Rear Subframe Mount Cracking
problems.affectedModels: All E82 variants, particularly high-mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on the E82 generation — the rear axle carrier panel can crack around the subframe mounting points under sustained load, especially on tuned or track-used cars.
problems.recommendation: Have this checked on any E82 over 80,000 miles — it's the single most common reason an otherwise sound E82 fails an inspection.
